Fig. 11. Comparison of spectral signatures for the central pixel (130, 750) under the 8× downsampling scale. Zoomed-in boxes are provided for detailed inspection of the reconstruction errors. y-axis: radiance; x-axis: band index.
Fig. 4. HADAR retrievals in the denoising experiment (σ = 1.0, s̃ = 0.1). Columns 1–3 show temperature (T , in K), emissivity (e), and normalized texture (X), respectively.

Fig. 8. HADAR retrievals in the inpainting experiment (b̃ = 0.5). Columns 1–3 show temperature (T , in K), emissivity (e), and normalized texture (X), respectively. The bottom panels provide zoomed-in views of the normalized texture X for HAIR and the ground truth HSI.

Fig. 9. Spectral calibration process. The uncalibrated observed sky signal s is aligned against a forward-modeled reference sr (Eq. 19) to compute the spectral shift map ∆λk (Eq. 20) and the calibrated sky signal ŝ(k) (Eq. 18).

Fig. 1. Flowchart of the HAIR framework. Given a degraded HSI Y , HAIR first extracts the observed sky signal and uses a simulated downwelling reference for spectral calibration (A-D). Then, HAIR uses the core TeX decompose-synthesize mechanism (E-F) to reconstruct a physics-consistent, noise-resilient Ytar.

Fig. 5. HADAR retrievals on real-world pushbroom imagery. Columns 1–3 show temperature (T , in K), emissivity (e), and normalized texture (X), respectively. The bottom panels provide zoomed-in views of the normalized texture X for HAIR and the original HSI.
Fig. 6. HADAR retrievals on real-world FTIR measurements. Rows 1–3 show temperature (T , in K), emissivity (e), and normalized texture (X), respectively.
Fig. 12. HADAR retrievals in the 8× spectral super-resolution experiment. Columns 1–3 show temperature (T , in K), emissivity (e), and normalized texture (X), respectively.
